title: data analyst path placement
description: A short quiz on the core ideas of the Data Analyst path. Answer what you know. Each chapter you place out of is one you can skip ahead on, and you can revisit any chapter any time.

scoring.chapterSkipRule: Each chapter is tested by 2 questions worth 2 points each. A learner places out of a chapter (skips its Version A) by answering both of its questions correctly. Overall, 50 percent or more of maxScore recommends Version B as the default for chapters not individually skipped.

questions.prompt: One row of a dataset is stored like this:
questions.codeQuestion: How do you get this row's sales number?

options.label: row["sales"]
row[3]
sales(row)
row.sales.value

questions.prompt: A dataset is often stored as a list of dictionaries. What does the first item in that list represent?

options.label: The first record, one full row of the data
The column headers only
The total number of rows
Nothing, lists cannot hold dictionaries

questions.prompt: You have a year of sales data in a CSV file and want to analyze it in code. What is the first step?

options.label: Open and read the file so its contents are available to your code
Email the file to yourself
Print the file out on paper
Nothing, code cannot read files

questions.prompt: You read a CSV file and the values come in as text, including the numbers. What is true?

options.label: You often need to convert text to numbers before you can do math on it
Text and numbers are the same to a computer, so math just works
A file can never contain numbers
You must retype the whole file by hand

questions.prompt: The data you need lives in another company's web service, not in a file you have. How do you pull it into your analysis?

options.label: Call their API, the web address that hands data back to a program
Screenshot their website and read the numbers off the image
You cannot use data unless it starts as a file on your computer
Ask them to mail you a printout

questions.prompt: When an API hands data back to your program, what form does that data usually take?

options.label: Structured text, commonly JSON, that your code can read field by field
A printed document
A spoken summary
A blank response, APIs never return data

questions.prompt: You have 1,000 messy free-text customer reviews and need each one turned into a sentiment rating and a topic, in columns you can analyze. What do you do?

options.label: Ask an AI model for structured output: a defined sentiment and topic field per review
Read all 1,000 yourself and type the columns by hand
Ask the model for a friendly paragraph about each review
Conclude free text cannot be analyzed

questions.prompt: Why does structured output matter for analysis work specifically?

options.label: It lands in consistent fields every time, so you can sort, count, and compute on it
It makes the AI model think harder
It does not matter, any text can be analyzed equally well
It is only useful for writing emails
